1: Amnon loved Tamar. The word ʾāhēḇ (love) is used for Amnon's feeling — the same vocabulary as covenant love — but the sequel shows this was not covenant love but consuming desire. The contrast with Christ's love for his bride is structural: 'Husbands, love your wives, as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her' (Ephesians 5:25). Amnon's 'love' takes; Christ's love gives. The chapter exposes, by contrast, what agapē requires and what desire-posing-as-love destroys.
2: Amnon was tormented because she was a virgin and it was impossible to do anything to her. The impossibility of access is the legal protection of Tamar's status as a virgin under her father's household. Christ, by contrast, makes access possible: 'I am the way, the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6). The barrier Amnon sought to break down illegitimately, Christ removes by his own self-giving — opening the way to the Father through the cross.
3: Jonadab was a very shrewd man (ḥākām məʾōd). Jonadab's cleverness — the wisdom of the world applied to sinful ends — is the inverse of the wisdom Christ gives: 'I will give you a mouth and wisdom which none of your adversaries will be able to withstand or contradict' (Luke 21:15). The NT contrast is between worldly cunning that enables harm and divine wisdom that serves truth. Christ is 'the wisdom of God' (1 Corinthians 1:24) — not cleverness in the service of desire but wisdom that endures.
4: Amnon grows thin day by day, consumed by desire. The consumption of the person by unchecked desire is what Paul addresses in Galatians 5:16-17: 'Walk by the Spirit, and you will not gratify the desires of the flesh.' Christ's call — 'Come to me, all who are weary and heavy-laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28) — addresses the same wasting: the rest Christ offers heals what unchecked desire destroys.
5: Jonadab's scheme: pretend to be ill, request Tamar's presence. The scheme uses the appearance of illness and family care as cover for assault. The deceptive scheme that uses false vulnerability to harm the innocent contrasts with Christ who takes on real vulnerability (the incarnation) precisely to serve the one who is genuinely helpless: 'when we were still weak, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly' (Romans 5:6).
6: Amnon lay down and pretended to be ill, and when the king came to visit, requested Tamar. David sends Tamar without apparent suspicion. The father's failure to protect the daughter through negligence or naivety contrasts with the Father who protects his own through the Son: 'I have guarded them and not one of them has been lost' (John 17:12). The good father does not send his children into harm; the Good Shepherd lays down his own life for the sheep rather than letting them be taken.
7: David sent to Tamar's house: Go to your brother Amnon's house. David's unwitting sending of Tamar into the house of the one who will harm her is the dark mirror of the Father sending the Son into the world. In both cases the one sent is innocent; the difference is that God the Father knew the danger fully and sent the Son willingly (John 3:16), while David was blind. The Father's sending is not negligence but infinite, knowing love.
8: Tamar came to Amnon's house and prepared the cakes in his sight. Her innocent, willing service — she has no knowledge of the scheme — is the service of one who has not been warned. Christ's disciples are warned by him of the danger that awaits: 'I am sending you out as sheep in the midst of wolves' (Matthew 10:16). Christ does not send without warning or without equipping; his servants are informed and accompanied, unlike Tamar.
9: Amnon refused to eat and had everyone sent out. The clearing of the house is the preparation for the assault under cover of privacy. Christ, by contrast, spoke openly: 'I have said nothing in secret' (John 18:20). The exposure vs. concealment contrast runs throughout the Johannine narrative — Christ's works are done in the light; the deeds of darkness require concealment.
10: Amnon said: Bring the food into the chamber, that I may eat from your hand. The movement into the inner chamber isolates Tamar. Christ's invitation is the opposite movement — from isolation to community, from the inner room of fear to the open table: 'I stand at the door and knock; if anyone opens the door, I will come in and eat with him' (Revelation 3:20). The one who is locked in with the one who will harm her stands in contrast to the one who is invited in to feast with the one who gives life.
11: Amnon took hold of her and said, Come, lie with me, my sister. The coercive demand — physical restraint combined with a command — is the opposite of Christ's invitation: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28). Christ never commands by coercion; his invitation is to those who freely choose to come. The contrast between Amnon's forcible taking and Christ's invitational giving marks the theological chasm between consuming desire and covenant love.
12: Tamar: No, my brother; do not violate me; such a thing is not done in Israel. Do not do this outrageous thing (nəḇālâ). Tamar names the act by its correct theological category — folly that violates covenant order. Jesus addresses the same root: 'from within, out of the heart, come evil thoughts, sexual immorality... all these evil things come from within and defile a person' (Mark 7:21-23). The nəḇālâ Tamar names comes from exactly the internal source Christ identifies.
13: Tamar says: Speak to the king, he will not withhold me from you. Tamar's appeal to the legitimate path — asking the king — is the counsel Christ provides for all desire: bring it to the Father in prayer (Matthew 7:7-8: ask and it will be given). The invitation to petition rather than seize is the OT's practical wisdom; Christ makes it explicit as the pattern of the kingdom: 'You do not have because you do not ask' (James 4:2).
14: He would not listen to her, and being stronger than she, he violated (ʿinnāh) her. The use of superior strength to overcome the weaker party is the opposite of Christ's use of power: 'Though he was in the form of God, he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself' (Philippians 2:6-7). Christ had all power and used none of it to seize; Amnon had limited power and used all of it to seize. The direction of power is the measure of love.
15: Amnon hated her with very great hatred, greater than his love. The exhaustion of desire in loathing is the signature of sin that mistakes lust for love. Christ's love is the inverse: 'having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end' (John 13:1). The Greek eis telos (to the end) is the deliberate contrast with love that ends in hatred: Christ's love for his people does not change character when it reaches its object; it intensifies.
16: Tamar: This wrong in sending me away is greater than the other. The dismissal after violation leaves Tamar without legal recourse — the destruction of her future compounded the destruction of her present. Christ never sends away those who come to him: 'All that the Father gives me will come to me, and whoever comes to me I will never cast out' (John 6:37). The one Amnon locked out of his house finds in Christ an open door that is never shut.
17: Amnon called his servant and had Tamar put out and the door bolted after her. The bolted door — nəʿal haḏdelet — excludes the violated woman from the place that had entrapped her. Christ at the closed door is the inverse: standing outside, knocking, asking for admission (Revelation 3:20). The one who was violently excluded from the house of harm is welcomed at the door of the one who heals.
18: Tamar was wearing a kəṯōneṯ passîm — the garment of the protected king's virgin daughter. The tearing of this garment in v19 is both literal and symbolic: what was designed to mark her status and protection is torn. The soldiers at the cross cast lots for Jesus's seamless tunic (John 19:23-24, citing Psalms 22:18) — both are innocent sufferers stripped of their distinctive garment. Tamar's torn garment and Christ's divided clothing both enact publicly what has been done to the innocent.
19: Tamar put ashes on her head and tore her robe and went away crying. Her public mourning is an act of witness — she does not hide the shame but makes it visible. Christ's ministry begins with addressing exactly this condition: 'He has sent me to give a beautiful headdress instead of ashes, the oil of gladness instead of mourning' (Isaiah 61:3, cited in Luke 4:18-19). Christ's first sermon in Nazareth declares his mission to those who sit in Tamar's condition — the violated, the mourning, the desolate.
20: Tamar lived, desolate (šōmēmâ), in Absalom's house. The word used for her condition is the same word used for the desolation of destroyed land. Christ's mission is the reversal of this desolation: 'Sing, O barren one, who did not bear; break forth into singing and cry aloud, you who have not been in labor! For the children of the desolate one will be more than the children of her who is married' (Isaiah 54:1). The desolate woman is the one to whom the Servant comes, and her desolation is turned to abundance.
21: David heard of all these things and was very angry, but he would not punish his son Amnon, because he loved him as his firstborn. David's failure to execute justice — anger without action — is the opposite of Christ's justice: 'He will not judge by what his eyes see, or decide disputes by what his ears hear, but with righteousness he shall judge the poor, and decide with equity for the meek of the earth' (Isaiah 11:3-4). Christ is the judge who will not fail to act; David is the king who did. Tamar's cry for justice found no human answer in her father; the eschatological judge is the answer to every unanswered justice-cry.
22: Absalom spoke to Amnon neither good nor bad — hating him in silence for two years. The calculated silence that harbors hatred for a planned revenge contrasts with the truth that Christ calls his disciples to: 'speaking the truth in love' (Ephesians 4:15). The failure to speak truth in the face of injustice — whether David's silence in v21 or Absalom's calculated silence here — leaves the wound unaddressed. Christ addresses injustice openly; his words are instruments of healing, not weapons of later revenge.
23: After two years, Absalom's plan ripens at the sheepshearing feast. The long patience before the strike and the choice of a festive occasion for the betrayal echoes the Gethsemane pattern: Judas's betrayal was arranged in advance and executed at the opportune moment (Luke 22:6: eukairia, a good opportunity). The contrast is that Judas betrayed the innocent Christ; Absalom avenged the violation of the innocent Tamar. The mechanism is similar; the moral direction is reversed.
24: Absalom came to David and invited him and all his sons to the sheepshearing. The repeated invitation to the father to attend — which David will ultimately decline (v25) — is the failed communication between a son who wants the father present and a father who will not come. Christ's invitation to the Father was perfectly answered: 'The Father has not left me alone, for I always do the things that are pleasing to him' (John 8:29). The Son who was never abandoned contrasts with Absalom who operated under the tacit neglect of a father who should have already acted.
25: David said, No, my son, let us not all go, lest we be burdensome to you. David's refusal to go — the father who will not show up — is a failure of presence. Christ's promise is the opposite of absence: 'I am with you always, to the end of the age' (Matthew 28:20). The disciples are not sent without the father's presence; the Good Shepherd does not send from a distance. David's benign excuse leaves Absalom operating without paternal oversight; Christ's presence with his disciples means they never act outside his covering.
26: Absalom specifically requests Amnon. David's question — why should he go? — shows a flicker of awareness but he does not pursue it. The father who suspects but does not investigate leaves the guilty free and the wronged unprotected. Christ does not turn away from the thing that needs to be addressed: 'he knew what was in man' (John 2:25) and did not leave it unspoken.
27: Absalom pressed him, and David permitted Amnon and all the king's sons to go. David's yielding to pressure — giving permission without wisdom — contributed to the outcome. Christ's decisions are not driven by social pressure: 'He did not entrust himself to them, because he knew all people' (John 2:24). He was not subject to the persuasive pressure of those around him but acted from clear knowledge of what was right.
28: Absalom commanded his servants to strike Amnon when his heart was merry with wine. The betrayal at the festive occasion — when the target was most off-guard — is the mechanism Judas also used (Matthew 26:14-16: the opportune moment when the crowd was not present). Both betray a companion at a meal; both are motivated by a grievance (Absalom's: the unaddressed violation; Judas's: the disputed allegiance). The pattern of betrayal at the meal is the OT shadow of the Lord's Supper and the betrayal that frames it.
29: All the king's sons arose and each mounted his mule and fled. The scattering of all the king's sons at the death of Amnon mirrors the scattering of the disciples at the arrest of Jesus: 'Then all the disciples left him and fled' (Matthew 26:56). In both narratives, the king's companions flee at the crisis; in both, their flight is followed eventually by reunion with the king. The scattered disciples return to Christ after the resurrection; David's scattered sons return to him after the initial panic (v34-36).
30: A false report reaches David: all the king's sons are dead, not one is left. The exaggerated report of total loss parallels the disciples' experience of Christ's death as total catastrophe — it looked like the end of everything. But in both cases the reality is less than the worst report: only one has died. From the disciples' side, the resurrection is the discovery that the worst report was false; the king is alive.
31: The king arose, tore his garments, lay on the ground, and all his servants stood by with their garments torn. The mourning posture — king and servants prostrate, garments torn — is the ritual response to apparent total loss. The community's shared mourning anticipates the disciples' grief after the crucifixion: 'They were looking sad' (Luke 24:17). The risen Christ meets mourners in their grief and transforms it; David's mourning is also eventually corrected by the news that the loss is limited.
32: Jonadab clarifies: only Amnon is dead. The correction of the worst-case report to a more limited truth is the structure of the resurrection message: 'Why do you seek the living among the dead? He is not here, but has risen' (Luke 24:5). The disciples expected total loss (all is over); the angel corrected the worst case with the actual truth. Jonadab — who had enabled the original crime — now provides the corrective information; the angel who delivers the resurrection news is the agent of pure grace.
33: Let not my lord the king take it to heart that all the king's sons are dead; only Amnon is dead. The repeated correction ensures the false report does not take root. Christ's appearances after the resurrection are similarly repetitive — multiple appearances to multiple witnesses — precisely because the disciples' despair required repeated evidence to overcome: 'He presented himself alive to them after his suffering by many proofs' (Acts 1:3).
34: Absalom fled; the watchman looked up and saw many people coming from the road by the side of the mountain. The return of the king's sons — visible from a distance, coming down the road — is the first sign of partial restoration after the crisis. The disciples on the Emmaus road are in the same position: unknown companion approaching on the road who turns out to be the key figure they thought lost (Luke 24:13-16). In both narratives, the one expected to be absent approaches from a distance.
35: Jonadab said: Behold, the king's sons have come; as your servant said, so it has come about. The word that was spoken has been confirmed — the prediction proved true. In the resurrection narrative, the angels' reminder ('he told you, while he was still in Galilee, that the Son of Man must be delivered... and rise', Luke 24:6-7) is the same confirmation: the word spoken beforehand has come about exactly as stated.
36: As Jonadab finished speaking, the king's sons came and lifted their voices and wept. And the king also and all his servants wept very bitterly. The reunion of the scattered sons with the king is accompanied by tears — grief and relief together. The disciples' reunion with the risen Christ has the same character: 'The disciples were glad when they saw the Lord' (John 20:20), but the reunion included recognition of the wounds, the reality of what had happened. Joy and grief are not opposites in either reunion.
37: Absalom fled to Geshur, to his maternal grandfather Talmai, and was there three years. David mourned for his son day after day. The three-year exile of the beloved (if wayward) son during which the father mourns is the temporal frame that intensifies the father's longing. Christ was absent from his disciples for a much shorter period (three days) but his return ended the mourning: 'Your sorrow will turn into joy' (John 16:20). The father's daily mourning for the absent son points to the Father's design for the Son's restoration.
38: So Absalom fled and went to Geshur, and was there three years. The three-year interval is the period of separation and the building of estrangement — Absalom will return physically but will remain spiritually separated from David for longer (2 Samuel 14-15). The prodigal son's estrangement from the father builds over time until the crisis of destitution (Luke 15:14-17); Absalom's estrangement from David deepens until the crisis of open rebellion (2 Samuel 15). Both endings of the estrangement are catastrophic; only one is redemptive.
39: The spirit of the king longed to go out to Absalom, because he was comforted about Amnon, since he was dead. David's consuming longing (watəḵal nēpeš-haMMeleḵ lāṣēṯ ʾel-ʾabšālôm) for the wayward, exiled son is the emotional content that Jesus puts into the father's running to meet the prodigal: 'while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and felt compassion, and ran and embraced him and kissed him' (Luke 15:20). The father who consumed with longing for his exiled son is David; the father who runs to meet the returning exile is the Father who receives all who return to him through Christ.
